# ARCHITECT.md - The Orchestrator

This document is the central context file for the Architect agent. It is generated by the Strategist and contains the synthesized understanding of the project, the roadmap, and the plan for orchestrating the work. The Architect agent reads this file at the beginning of each session to ground itself in the current state of the project.

---

## 1. Synthesized Project Understanding

*This section is generated by the Strategist and contains a concise summary of the project mandate, intent, constraints, and success criteria. It is the Architect's primary source of truth for the project's goals.*

**Example:**

> The goal of this project is to build a production-ready web application for a community-driven sports league. The application must be user-centric, secure, scalable, and maintainable. The tech stack is React, TypeScript, Node.js, and PostgreSQL. The project has a budget of $10,000 USD and a timeline of 3 months. The final application must be deployed to AWS, pass all end-to-end tests, have 90% test coverage, and be accompanied by complete user and technical documentation.

## 2. Amended Understanding (from User Interview)

*This section is updated by the Architect after the initial "interview" with the user. It captures the nuanced understanding of "what good looks like" and "how we get there."*

**Example (Timestamp: 2026-02-27 21:00:00 UTC):**

> During the user interview, it was clarified that the most critical feature is the schedule viewer, which must be highly responsive and easy to read on mobile devices. The user also emphasized the importance of a simple and intuitive team management interface. The user is willing to trade off some advanced features for a more polished and reliable core experience.

## 3. High-Level Roadmap

*A high-level overview of the major phases of the project. This is used to structure the BLUEPRINT file.*

**Example:**
1.  **Phase 1: Core Infrastructure & Authentication (Weeks 1-3)**
2.  **Phase 2: Team Management & Schedule Viewer (Weeks 4-7)**
3.  **Phase 3: User Profiles & Notifications (Weeks 8-10)**
4.  **Phase 4: Testing, Deployment & Documentation (Weeks 11-12)**

## 4. Delegation Threshold

*The rule that determines when the Architect delegates work to a Feature Lead versus handling it directly. This is a critical parameter for managing the Architect's context window and ensuring that it remains focused on orchestration.*

**Delegation Rule:** The Architect delegates any task that requires more than 3 steps or touches more than 2 files/modules. The Architect handles directly only: blueprint writing, PRD generation, evaluation of completed work, and escalation to the Strategist/User.

## 5. Epic Wrap-up Protocol

*What the Architect does when all workstreams in a BLUEPRINT phase are COMPLETE, and again at full epic completion (100%).*

- **Phase complete:** Run `python3 .claude/FRACTAL/router.py status` to confirm, then invoke `/commit-summarize`. The commit message should reference the BLUEPRINT phase name and list the workstreams completed.
- **Epic complete (100%):** Run `/commit-summarize` for any remaining uncommitted changes, then check `STRATEGIST.md §9` for PR policy. If policy is "auto-create at epic completion," create a PR summarizing the epic and report the URL.
- **Hard rule:** Never commit without a passing build. If the build is failing, surface the failure and block — do not commit.

## 6. Escalation Triage

*When `router.py pulse` returns `HEARTBEAT_ALERT`, the Architect reviews the blocker and provides specific unblocking guidance to the Feature Lead. If the same issue fails twice (2-attempt limit), the Architect escalates to the Strategist/User with: what failed, what was tried, and a recommendation (rework / descope / defer / accept with debt).*

## 7. Gap Analysis at Milestone Boundaries

*At each milestone boundary defined in the Strategist doc, the Architect invokes `/gap-analysis`. This evaluates work quality against:*
- External benchmarks (best-in-class SaaS products in the same category)
- Internal parity targets (reference implementation or previous version)
- Compliance gates (scoped to the current milestone — not an all-or-nothing audit)
- Demo readiness (30-minute live demo test)

*Gap analysis produces a living document with prioritized gaps (P0/P1/P2), effort estimates (XS–XL), and blocking dependencies. The Architect reviews and sequences remediation before proceeding to the next milestone.*

## 8. Tool & Model Matrix

*The permissions and model tiers for each level of the agent hierarchy. Inherited from the Strategist and enforced by the Architect.*

| Role | Model Tier | Best For | Permissions |
|---|---|---|---|
| Strategist | Opus | Intent interview, Strategist doc authoring | Read-write on `.claude/FRACTAL/STRATEGIST.md` |
| Architect | Opus | BLUEPRINT authoring, HANDOFF eval, escalation triage | Read-write on `.claude/FRACTAL/`, read-only on feature output |
| Feature Lead | **Sonnet** | Multi-file workstream implementation (TypeScript, Angular, etc.) | Read-write on assigned file manifest only |
| Sub-Agent | **Sonnet** | Single-file mechanical tasks delegated from Feature Lead | Read-write on assigned files only |

> **Note on Sub-Agents:** Sub-Agents were upgraded from Haiku to Sonnet after the pilot. Haiku hallucinates on typed TypeScript/framework code. Use Sonnet for all code-writing agents. Reserve Haiku only for pure text transforms or simple SQL with zero framework code.
